compressed_prompt: Describe tennis balls in medieval verse, P.P.S. modern materials.
response:
O, fair tennis ball, thy wondrous might,
In pressurized delight, doth take the light.
A fuzzy coat, of felt so fine and bright,
Doth encase the inner workings, a wondrous sight.

Thy rubber core, a marvel of modern art,
Doth yield to pressure, and doth play its part.
The air within, compressed with skill and care,
Doth make thee bounce, with vigor beyond compare.

Thy surface, textured, with a fuzzy sheen,
Doth grip the racket, and doth make the ball be seen.
The felt, a blend of wool and synthetic might,
Doth provide the traction, for a shot so bright.

In days of yore, thou wouldst have been made of wood,
Or leather, stitched with care, as was the custom good.
But now, in modern times, thou art crafted with ease,
From materials new, that bring thee to thy knees.

Thy pressurized core, a marvel of our age,
Doth make thee bounce, with a lively stage.
The rubber, synthetic, doth provide the might,
To withstand the forces, of a serve so bright.

P.P.S. In sooth, fair ball, thy modern guise,
Doth boast a pressurized core, of rubber, wise.
The felt, a mix of wool and synthetic thread,
Doth provide the grip, that doth make the players' hearts be glad.

Thus, in thy modern form, thou dost delight,
The tennis players, with thy bouncy might.
May thy fuzzy coat, and pressurized core,
Bring joy to the game, forevermore.
